end of world model.

Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 77 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 2.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the pattern clearly:
1. Transitions 4-7: RIGHT actions moved the charcoal F shape (c: 45→18→18→36→45) and affected the sky blue strip (S: 189→117→135→135→189) and blue background (b: 1800→1827→1818→1809→1800). This seems like the F shape was moving back and forth.
2. Transitions 57-75: SPACE actions decreased the yellow F shape pixel count from 45 down to 1. Each SPACE removes 1 pixel from the yellow F.
Current state: Y=1 (yellow F has only 1 pixel left), c=45 (charcoal F is full), S=189 (sky blue strip is full), b=1800 (blue background is full).
So SPACE is eroding the yellow F shape. The goal might be to completely remove the yellow F by pressing SPACE until it's gone (Y=0).
Let me check what happens when Y reaches 0. I should press SPACE one more time.

print("\nCurrent state nodes:")

for n in cur_seg:
    print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']} children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I understand the game much better!
Initial state:
- Blue background (b) split into left and right panels
- Sky blue vertical strip (S) in the middle (cols 30-32)
- Yellow vertical bar (Y) on the right edge (id=3, 64 pixels) - this is the "progress bar"
- Black F shape (B, id=4) in the left panel with 5 white pixels inside (the F cutout)
- Charcoal F shape (c, id=5) in the right panel
- Yellow F shape (Y, id=11) in the right panel
- Black bottom bar (B, id=12)
Current state:
- The black F shape has moved to the right edge and now contains the white pixels (id=3, B=103)
- The charcoal F shape (c) is on the left edge
- The yellow F shape (Y, id=8) is still in the right panel
- A single yellow pixel (Y, id=12) is at [63, 63] - the bottom right corner
- The right edge black bar has shrunk from 64 to 1 pixel (the corner)
So the game mechanics seem to be:
1. The black F shape on the right edge acts as a "progress" indicator - it started at 64 pixels (full height) and has been eroded down to just 1 pixel.
2. SPACE actions erode the yellow F shape (id=8) - it went from 45 to 1 pixel.
3. RIGHT actions seem to move things around.
Wait, let me reconsider. Looking at the current state:
- The yellow F shape (id=8) still has 45 pixels - it hasn't changed!
- The single yellow pixel (id=12) at [63, 63] is what's been eroding from 64→1.
So the yellow bar on the right edge (id=3 initially, now id=12) is the progress bar. SPACE actions decrement it. It started at 64 and is now at 1.
The goal is probably to get it to 0 (or some target). Let me press SPACE one more time to see if it completes the level.
Actually wait - looking more carefully:
- Initial: Y=64 (id=3) and Y=45 (id=11) - total Y=109
- Current: Y=45 (id=8) and Y=1 (id=12) - total Y=46
So the right-edge yellow bar went from 64 to 1 (63 SPACE presses). The yellow F shape stayed at 45.
If the goal is to get the bar to 0, I need one more SPACE. Let me try that.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The right-edge yellow bar has gone from 64 to 1 pixel. 
# One more SPACE should bring it to 0 and potentially complete the level.
action(['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
